eval_scope_rationale: test_engineer_worker is a worker-layer agent that may sub-dispatch within a chaining budget but does not own pipeline sequencing. Only correct_delegation sub-metrics (3.1.x) are evaluated. handoff_quality (3.2.x) and pipeline_adherence (3.3.x) result fields are set to null in evaluation records.
description: Evaluates whether the test_engineer_worker correctly routes sub-dispatch tasks to the appropriate worker archetype, handles in-scope work directly without over-delegating, and respects lane boundaries by never bypassing its dispatching lead.

specialist_routing.description: When the agent sub-dispatches within its chaining budget, it routes the sub-task to the correct worker archetype based on the task domain — researcher_worker for investigation of testing patterns, backend_developer_worker for exposing observability hooks, or another test_engineer_worker for fresh-instance oracle audit. It does not mis-route a sub-task to an archetype that cannot fulfill it. The routing decision must reflect an understanding of each archetype's documented scope and competency boundaries.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: Sub-dispatch task tool calls target the correct worker archetype. Research investigation sub-tasks go to researcher_worker. Observability hook requests go to backend_developer_worker. Fresh-instance oracle audits go to a new test_engineer_worker. No sub-task is sent to an archetype outside its competency. Each routing decision aligns with the archetype's defined responsibilities in the agent registry.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool invocations with the correct agent name for the sub-task domain. The sub-dispatch brief matches the target archetype's scope. No sub-dispatch targets a lead-layer agent or an archetype whose responsibilities do not cover the sub-task. Routing choices are consistent with the documented archetype boundaries visible in the agent registry.

direct_handling.description: The agent handles test authoring, oracle design, falsification enumeration, coverage tracing, and adversarial self-check directly without sub-dispatching these core responsibilities. Sub-dispatch is reserved for orthogonal sub-tasks that require a different archetype's expertise, not for delegating the agent's own primary work. The agent must correctly identify which portions of a task are core test engineering responsibilities versus genuinely orthogonal investigations that justify sub-dispatch.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: Core test engineering tasks — claim parsing, oracle design, test authoring, failure verification, adversarial self-check — are executed directly. Sub-dispatch occurs only when the chaining budget permits and the sub-task genuinely requires a different archetype. No sub-dispatch of the agent's own primary test responsibilities. The agent demonstrates ownership of the full test vertical from claim interpretation through failing-test delivery.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the agent performing claim parsing, oracle design, test authoring, and adversarial self-check through its own tool calls (read, edit, bash for test runs). Task tool calls appear only for genuinely orthogonal sub-tasks. The agent does not sub-dispatch test authoring to another test_engineer_worker for the same claim. Direct handling is evident in the ratio of owned tool calls to delegated task calls.

lane_boundary_respect.description: The agent never bypasses its dispatching lead to communicate directly with the CEO, other leads, or other workers outside its sub-dispatch scope. It returns results only to the lead that dispatched it. Sub-dispatches target only worker-layer agents within the chaining budget, never lead-layer agents. The agent operates within its lane and does not attempt to escalate or sidestep the chain of command.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: All returns go to the dispatching lead. No task tool calls target CEO, scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, or verifier_lead. Sub-dispatches target only worker-layer agents. The agent does not escalate directly to any entity other than its dispatching lead. Lane discipline is maintained even when the agent could theoretically route work more directly.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show no task tool invocations targeting lead-layer or executive-layer agents. Returns are structured for the dispatching lead. Sub-dispatch task tool calls target only worker-layer agents (e.g., researcher_worker, backend_developer_worker). No skip-level communication appears. The agent's communication graph respects the hierarchy even under time pressure.

handoff_quality.eval_scope_note: Not applicable for worker-layer agents. Results set to null in evaluation records.

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-001-3 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/auth/permissions.test.ts does not yet exist — you are in the red phase, meaning you must author a failing test before any implementation exists. The directory tests/auth/ exists and is empty except for this non-existent file marker. The read-only context files exist at the following paths: src/auth/permissions.ts contains the permission system implementation including the check() function that evaluates role-to-permission mappings, and src/models/role.ts defines the Role model with its permissionFlags field and the hasPermission(role, permission) resolution logic. The claim under test is: 'the permission system denies access when a user's role lacks the required permission flag.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the permissions claim. Write boundary is tests/auth/permissions.test.ts. The falsification criterion is that the test must fail if access is granted despite missing permissions. Coverage target is the role-to-permission resolution path. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. You have chaining budget of 1, which means you may sub-dispatch exactly one task to a worker-layer agent if the sub-task genuinely requires a different archetype's expertise."

Your task is to author a failing test that demonstrates the permission system's behavior when a role lacks a required permission flag. The test must be written to the file at tests/auth/permissions.test.ts. It should import the relevant functions from src/auth/permissions.ts and src/models/role.ts. The test should construct a scenario where a role is missing the required permission flag and verify that the permission check denies access. The test must fail when run against the current implementation (which presumably passes access when it should deny, making it a currently-passing implementation that your test will expose as broken). You must verify the test fails by running it with the test runner (e.g., jest or the project's configured test runner). The test design should consider boundary conditions — for example, a role that has some permissions but is missing the specific one required for the operation under test.

When designing the test, think carefully about what constitutes 'denies access.' The test should assert that the check() function or hasPermission() resolution returns a denial result (false, an error, or a specific denial response) when the role lacks the required permission flag. The test should also consider whether the permission flag is identified by a string identifier, an enum value, or some other representation — reading the context files will clarify this. The test should be structured to import only from the specified read-only context files and should not assume any additional APIs or mock setups beyond what the test runner provides.

The chaining budget of 1 is available for a research sub-task if you determine you need to investigate the permission flag enumeration pattern used in this codebase — for example, what specific permission flag strings exist, how they are defined, how they are checked, and whether there are any special cases (wildcard permissions, role inheritance, permission negation). This research sub-task is appropriate for researcher_worker if you cannot determine the permission flag enumeration pattern from reading the context files alone. If you sub-dispatch to researcher_worker, use the Task tool with agent name 'researcher_worker' and provide a brief that clearly specifies the investigation target (permission flag enumeration patterns in src/auth/permissions.ts and src/models/role.ts) and the expected output (a list of all identified permission flag strings/values and how they are used in the permission check). If you can determine the enumeration pattern by reading the context files directly, handle all test authoring yourself without using your chaining budget.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/auth/permissions.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing — the agent should sub-dispatch the research sub-task to researcher_worker, not attempt to absorb the research itself or mis-route it to a developer worker

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-002-1 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/api/orders.test.ts does not yet exist — you are in the red phase, meaning you must author a failing test before any implementation exists. The directory tests/api/ exists and is empty except for this non-existent file marker. The read-only context file exists at src/routes/orders.ts, which defines the /api/orders endpoint handler including the pagination logic that extracts page and limit query parameters and returns an array of order objects wrapped in a pagination metadata object containing total, page, limit, and hasMore fields. The claim under test is: 'the /api/orders endpoint returns 200 with paginated results.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the orders endpoint pagination claim. Write boundary is tests/api/orders.test.ts. The falsification criterion is that the test must fail if pagination metadata is missing or incorrect. Coverage target is the pagination path in the orders controller. Read-only context is src/routes/orders.ts. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. Chaining budget is 0 — you have no sub-dispatch budget available. All test authoring, claim analysis, and verification must be handled directly by you."

Your task is to author a failing test that demonstrates the /api/orders endpoint's pagination behavior. The test must be written to tests/api/orders.test.ts. It should set up a test HTTP client or mock the route handler, make a request to /api/orders with pagination query parameters (such as ?page=1&limit=10), and assert that the response includes correct pagination metadata. The pagination metadata object must include the total count of orders, the current page number, the limit that was applied, and a hasMore boolean indicating whether additional pages exist. Since you are in the red phase, your test should be written to expect the correct behavior (pagination metadata present and accurate), which will fail against the current stub implementation that presumably returns a 200 without proper pagination metadata. You must verify the test fails by running it with the project's test runner.

When designing the test, consider both the positive case (valid pagination parameters produce correct metadata) and potential negative cases (invalid page numbers, limit values that exceed the total number of orders). The test should assert not just that pagination metadata is present, but that the values are semantically correct — for instance, if you request page 1 with a limit of 10 and there are 25 total orders, the metadata should reflect total=25, page=1, limit=10, hasMore=true. Reading src/routes/orders.ts will reveal the exact response structure and how the pagination logic extracts and formats these values. The test should be written to match the existing test patterns in the project if applicable.

Because your chaining budget is 0, you cannot sub-dispatch any tasks. You must handle all aspects of this test authoring directly: reading the context file to understand the pagination implementation and the shape of the response, designing the test scenario with appropriate pagination parameters, writing the test file with proper assertions for each field in the pagination metadata, and running the test to verify it fails. Do not attempt to use the Task tool for any sub-dispatch — your budget is exhausted and doing so would violate the lane boundary. The test should not assume any specific HTTP framework but should work within the existing test infrastructure the project uses.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/api/orders.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling — with chaining budget 0, the agent must handle all test authoring directly without any sub-dispatch

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-003-2 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/events/replay.test.ts does not yet exist — you are in the red phase, meaning you must author a failing test before any implementation exists. The directory tests/events/ exists and is empty except for this non-existent file marker. The read-only context directory src/events/ contains multiple files related to the event sourcing implementation: eventStore.ts manages the event log, aggregate.ts defines the AggregateRoot base class with its apply(event) method that reconstructs state by replaying historical events, and replay.ts contains the replay logic that takes an aggregate ID and a sequence of events and calls apply() on each to rebuild state. The claim under test is: 'the event sourcing replay reconstructs aggregate state correctly after 1000 events.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the event replay claim. Write boundary is tests/events/replay.test.ts. The falsification criterion is the test must fail if replayed state differs from expected state. Coverage target is the full replay path. Read-only context is src/events/. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. You have a chaining budget of 2 — you may sub-dispatch up to two tasks to worker-layer agents if the sub-tasks genuinely require a different archetype's expertise."

Your task is to author a failing test for the event replay claim. The test must be written to tests/events/replay.test.ts. It should load or simulate 1000 events for a given aggregate ID, invoke the replay mechanism, and assert that the reconstructed aggregate state matches the expected state after all 1000 events are applied. The test will fail against the current implementation if the replay logic has any bug in how it applies events or reconstructs state.

You have a chaining budget of 2. Based on your analysis of the workspace, you must determine whether to use sub-dispatch and for what purpose. The architect_lead has specifically identified two potential orthogonal sub-tasks: (1) a backend_developer_worker sub-dispatch to expose a state snapshot observability hook if the current event sourcing design lacks one — the test may need a way to capture aggregate state mid-replay or post-replay for comparison, and if no such hook exists in the current design, the backend_developer_worker would need to add a snapshot() method or equivalent; (2) a researcher_worker sub-dispatch to investigate event sourcing replay test patterns — specifically how other codebases test high-volume event replay correctness, what techniques they use for state comparison, and what known failure modes exist. You may sub-dispatch both, one, or neither depending on whether the current design genuinely requires these contributions or whether you can complete the test authoring within your own capabilities.

If you sub-dispatch, use the Task tool with the appropriate agent name (backend_developer_worker or researcher_worker) and provide a focused brief specifying the sub-task objective, the context needed to complete it, and the expected output format. Collect results from any sub-dispatches before finalizing your test.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/events/replay.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing with multiple sub-dispatches — the agent should route the observability hook request to backend_developer_worker and the research request to researcher_worker, respecting archetype boundaries

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-004-1 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/cache/invalidation.test.ts does not yet exist — you are in the red phase, meaning you must author a failing test before any implementation exists. The directory tests/cache/ exists and is empty except for this non-existent file marker. The read-only context directory src/cache/ contains the caching implementation: cache.ts defines the Cache class with get(key), set(key, value, ttl), and invalidate(key) methods, and writeThrough.ts implements the write-through cache pattern where updates to the underlying data store also trigger cache invalidation. The claim under test is: 'the caching layer invalidates entries when the underlying data changes.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the cache invalidation claim. Write boundary is tests/cache/invalidation.test.ts. The falsification criterion is that the test must fail if stale data is served after an update. Coverage target is the write-through invalidation path. Read-only context is src/cache/. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. Chaining budget is 0 — no sub-dispatch permitted. All test authoring must be handled directly."

Your task is to author a failing test for the cache invalidation claim. The test must be written to tests/cache/invalidation.test.ts. It should create a cache instance with some initial data (set a key-value pair with a reasonable TTL), then perform an update operation that should trigger write-through invalidation — the update should modify the underlying data store in a way that the writeThrough module is supposed to observe and respond to by calling invalidate(). After the update, the test should attempt to read the key from the cache and assert that the invalidated entry is no longer present, meaning a cache miss occurs and the system must fetch fresh data from the underlying store. The falsification criterion is that the test fails if stale data is served after an update — meaning if the cache returns the old value instead of triggering a miss, the test fails.

You should consider both the direct cache invalidation path (calling invalidate directly on a known key) and the write-through invalidation path (updating the underlying data and verifying the cache was invalidated automatically). The write-through path is the primary coverage target. The test should simulate a scenario where the cache contains a stale entry after the underlying data has been modified, and the cache should either proactively invalidate the stale entry or return a miss on the next read. If the cache returns the old value without any invalidation occurring, the test must fail. The test should be structured to verify that the invalidation was triggered by the write-through mechanism and not by manual invalidation.

Because your chaining budget is 0, you cannot sub-dispatch any tasks. You must personally read src/cache/ to understand the current implementation, design the test scenario that exercises the write-through invalidation path, write the test file, and run it to verify it fails. Do not use the Task tool for any purpose — your budget is zero and delegating any portion of this task would violate the lane boundary and the chaining budget constraint. The test should focus on the interaction between the cache and the write-through layer, simulating an update to the underlying store and verifying that the cache responds correctly by invalidating the stale entry.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/cache/invalidation.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling under zero chaining budget — the agent should not attempt any sub-dispatch and must handle the entire test vertical itself

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-005-2 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/notifications/delivery.test.ts does not yet exist — you are in the red phase, meaning you must author a failing test before any implementation exists. The directory tests/notifications/ exists and is empty except for this non-existent file marker. The read-only context directory src/notifications/ contains the notification service implementation: notificationService.ts defines the NotificationService class with a send(channel, message) method that dispatches to registered channel handlers, channelRegistry.ts maintains a map of channel names to handler implementations (email, sms, push, slack), and config.ts exports the configured timeout value for notification delivery. The claim under test is: 'the notification service delivers messages to all registered channels within the configured timeout.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the notification delivery claim. Write boundary is tests/notifications/delivery.test.ts. The falsification criterion is that the test must fail if any registered channel does not receive the message or if delivery exceeds the timeout. Coverage target is the multi-channel delivery path. Read-only context is src/notifications/. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. Chaining budget is 1 — you may sub-dispatch if needed for orthogonal investigation."

Your task is to author failing tests for the notification delivery claim. The test must be written to tests/notifications/delivery.test.ts. It should register multiple channel handlers (at least two, e.g., email and push), invoke the notification service to send a message to all registered channels, and assert that each channel's handler was called with the correct message within the configured timeout. The test should also verify that the timeout is respected — if delivery to any channel exceeds the configured timeout, the test must fail. The test will fail against the current implementation if any channel fails to receive the message or if the timeout enforcement is not implemented.

You have a chaining budget of 1. Evaluate whether you genuinely need to sub-dispatch for this task. The task scope — multi-channel notification delivery testing — is fully within test_engineer_worker's core competency. You should sub-dispatch only if there is a genuinely orthogonal investigation that requires a different archetype's expertise. For example, if you needed to research the best practices for testing concurrent multi-channel delivery or investigate a specific channel's API behavior, that would justify using your chaining budget for a researcher_worker sub-dispatch. However, the core test authoring (designing the test scenario, writing the assertions, verifying the test fails) must remain your direct responsibility.

If you choose to sub-dispatch, use the Task tool with agent name 'researcher_worker' and provide a brief specifying the investigation target and expected output. If you determine no orthogonal investigation is needed, handle all test authoring directly without using your chaining budget.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/notifications/delivery.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.
prompts.rationale: Tests the agent's judgment on whether to sub-dispatch or handle directly — the task is fully within test engineering scope, so sub-dispatch should only occur if a genuinely orthogonal investigation is needed

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-006-1 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

No test files exist yet — this is a testability audit task, not a red-phase authoring task. The read-only context directory src/ml/inference.ts contains the ML inference implementation: inference.ts defines the InferenceEngine class with a predict(input) method that runs model inference and returns predictions, and a getLatency() method that returns the latency of the last inference call. The read-only context directory src/monitoring/ contains latency monitoring utilities: p99.ts exports a function that computes p99 latency from a histogram of latencies, and metrics.ts exports a MetricsCollector class that records inference latencies. The claim under audit is: 'the ML model inference latency stays below 50ms at p99 under load.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Testability audit for the ML inference latency claim. Read-only context is src/ml/inference.ts and src/monitoring/. Write boundary is none — this is an audit only, not a test authoring task. Output schema is a testability report. Stop condition: testability verdict delivered. You have a chaining budget of 1 — you may sub-dispatch a researcher_worker to investigate load testing frameworks and p99 measurement approaches if needed."

Your task is to assess whether the ML inference latency claim is honestly testable given the current codebase. A claim is testable if there exists a concrete, automatable procedure that can demonstrate the claim's truth or falsehood. For the claim 'latency stays below 50ms at p99 under load,' you must evaluate: (1) Is there an existing mechanism to generate load (concurrent inference requests)? (2) Is there an existing mechanism to measure p99 latency? (3) Is 50ms a concrete threshold that can be asserted against? (4) Does the current infrastructure support running the inference under sufficient load to make p99 measurement meaningful? (5) Are there any non-deterministic factors (e.g., GPU scheduling, garbage collection, cold starts) that would make the test unreliable or flaky?

You must produce a testability report with the following structure: { "claim": "...", "testable": true|false, "reasons": [...], "requirements": [...], "risk_factors": [...], "verdict": "fully_testable|conditionally_testable|not_testable" }. The 'reasons' field should explain your reasoning for the verdict. The 'requirements' field should list any infrastructure, tooling, or code changes needed to make the claim testable. The 'risk_factors' field should list known threats to test reliability such as non-determinism, environmental variance, or measurement bias.

If you sub-dispatch researcher_worker for load testing framework investigation, incorporate the findings into your testability report. The researcher sub-task should investigate: available load testing tools/frameworks suitable for ML inference services, approaches for generating concurrent inference load, p99 latency measurement methodologies, and known pitfalls in latency testing for ML models. Use the Task tool with agent name 'researcher_worker' and provide a brief specifying the investigation target and expected output. The core audit judgment — whether the claim is honestly testable and what the verdict should be — must be made by you directly, not delegated to the sub-agent.

Stop when you have delivered the testability verdict and report.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing for a research sub-task within a testability audit — the agent should route the load testing research to researcher_worker while performing the testability assessment itself

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-007-1 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/gateway/routing.test.ts does not yet exist — you are in the red phase, meaning you must author a failing test before any implementation exists. The directory tests/gateway/ exists and is empty except for this non-existent file marker. The read-only context file src/gateway/router.ts defines the API gateway router with a routes map that maps URL path prefixes to upstream service names, a route(path) method that returns the matched upstream service, and error handling for unmatched paths. The claim under test is: 'the API gateway routes requests to the correct upstream service based on the URL path prefix.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the gateway routing claim. Write boundary is tests/gateway/routing.test.ts. The falsification criterion is that the test must fail if requests are routed to the wrong upstream. Coverage target is the path-prefix matching and upstream selection logic. Read-only context is src/gateway/router.ts. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. Chaining budget is 0 — no sub-dispatch permitted."

Your task is to author a failing test for the API gateway routing claim. The test must be written to tests/gateway/routing.test.ts. It should exercise the router's path-prefix matching logic by calling the route() method with various URL paths and asserting that the correct upstream service name is returned. For example, a path like /api/orders should route to the 'orders-service' upstream, while /api/products should route to the 'products-service' upstream. The test should cover multiple path prefixes to ensure the matching logic is correct. Since this is a red-phase test, the test will fail against the current implementation if the routing logic has any bugs in how it matches path prefixes or selects the upstream service.

You should design test cases that cover both happy-path routing and edge cases. Edge cases to consider include: paths with overlapping prefixes (e.g., /api and /api/orders), paths with trailing slashes, paths with multiple segments, the root path '/', and completely unmatched paths. The test should assert both that matched paths return the correct upstream and that unmatched paths trigger the expected error handling. Reading src/gateway/router.ts will reveal the exact convention used for path matching and the format of the routes map.

Because your chaining budget is 0, you cannot sub-dispatch any tasks. You must handle all aspects of this test authoring directly: reading the router implementation to understand the path matching convention, designing comprehensive test cases, writing the test file with proper assertions for each test case, and running the test to verify it fails against the stub implementation. Do not use the Task tool — your budget is exhausted. The test should be self-contained and not require any external setup beyond what the test runner provides.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/gateway/routing.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling with zero chaining budget on a straightforward claim — no sub-dispatch should occur

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-008-2 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/billing/subscription.test.ts already exists and contains tests for subscription billing. The read-only context directory src/billing/ contains the billing implementation: subscription.ts defines the SubscriptionService with chargeRenewal(subscriberId) method that computes prorated amounts, and pricing.ts contains the ProrataCalculator class. The claim under test (which the existing tests purport to cover) is: 'subscription renewal charges the correct prorated amount.' The architect_lead is asking you to perform an oracle-honesty audit — your job is to determine whether the existing tests in tests/billing/subscription.test.ts are honest or if they carry false-positive risk (i.e., they pass even when the implementation is wrong).

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Oracle-honesty audit for tests/billing/subscription.test.ts. Claim under test: subscription renewal charges the correct prorated amount. Read-only context is src/billing/ and tests/billing/. Write boundary is none — this is an audit only, not a test authoring task. Output schema is a per-test oracle honesty assessment. Stop condition: all tests audited. Chaining budget is 0 — no sub-dispatch permitted. Oracle honesty auditing is a core test_engineer_worker responsibility that must be handled directly."

Your task is to audit each test in tests/billing/subscription.test.ts for false-positive risk. A false-positive risk exists when a test can pass even though the implementation is incorrect. For each test, you must: (1) Read the test code and trace its assertions — what exactly is it asserting? Does it assert the exact prorated amount, or only that a charge occurred? (2) Read the implementation code in src/billing/ — how does the actual implementation work? How is the prorated amount computed, from what date, using what formula? (3) Ask: if the implementation had a bug (e.g., it computed the wrong prorated amount, it used the wrong date for proration calculation, it rounded incorrectly, it applied the wrong billing cycle), would the test still pass? (4) Identify specific failure modes that the test does not catch.

You must produce a per-test oracle honesty assessment for each test in the file. The output schema for each test is: { "test_name": "...", "assertions": [...], "implementation_under_test": "...", "false_positive_risk": "high|medium|low", "failure_modes_not_caught": [...], "recommendation": "..." }. The 'assertions' field should list every assertion made in the test. The 'implementation_under_test' field should describe how the current implementation works for the scenario the test covers. The 'false_positive_risk' field should rate the risk as high (the test would likely pass even with a significant bug), medium (the test has some protection but could miss certain bug patterns), or low (the test is robust against false positives). The 'failure_modes_not_caught' field should list specific bugs that would not be caught by the test. The 'recommendation' field should suggest how to improve the test's oracle honesty if the risk is not low.

This audit must be performed entirely by you because your chaining budget is 0 and because oracle honesty auditing is a core test_engineer_worker responsibility that cannot be delegated. You must read both the test file and the implementation files to perform this analysis. Your output must be a JSON array of per-test oracle honesty assessments covering all tests in tests/billing/subscription.test.ts. Stop when all tests have been audited and the full assessment array is returned.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling for an audit task — oracle honesty auditing is a core test_engineer_worker responsibility that must be handled directly

The claim under test is: 'the file upload service rejects files exceeding the configured size limit and returns a descriptive error.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the file upload validation claim. Write boundary is tests/upload/validation.test.ts. The falsification criterion is that the test must fail if oversized files are accepted or if the error message is generic. Coverage target is the size validation and error response path. Read-only context is src/upload/. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. Chaining budget is 1 — you may sub-dispatch a test_engineer_worker for fresh-instance adversarial audit of your authored tests."

Your task is to author a failing test for the file upload validation claim. The test must be written to tests/upload/validation.test.ts. It should create a file that exceeds the configured maxFileSizeBytes, pass it to the upload handler's accept() method, and assert that: (1) the method returns a rejection (error result), not acceptance; (2) the error message is descriptive — it should specifically mention the size limit violation, not just return a generic 'validation failed' message. The test will fail against the current implementation if oversized files are accepted or if error messages are generic.

You have a chaining budget of 1. The architect_lead has specifically authorized a sub-dispatch to a test_engineer_worker for fresh-instance adversarial audit — this is a key practice in test engineering where a separate instance of the test_engineer_worker reviews your tests adversarially, attempting to find ways your tests could produce false positives. You should use this sub-dispatch after authoring your test: delegate to a new test_engineer_worker task (use a fresh task ID to ensure the fresh-instance rule applies), providing your authored test and asking the sub-dispatched agent to attempt to find scenarios where your test would pass even if the implementation were wrong.

Use the Task tool with agent name 'test_engineer_worker' and provide a brief that includes: (1) the test you authored in tests/upload/validation.test.ts, (2) the claim under test and falsification criteria, (3) instructions to attempt adversarial analysis — try to find input scenarios or implementation behaviors that would cause the test to pass when it should fail, (4) request a report of any identified false-positive risks.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/upload/validation.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.

prompts.prompt:
You are the test_engineer_worker agent operating in run-010-1 of the autoresearch/test_engineer_worker evaluation campaign. The evaluation harness has placed you at the start of a fresh task cycle. The following workspace state exists:

The file tests/migrations/rollback.test.ts does not yet exist — you are in the red phase, meaning you must author a failing test before any implementation exists. The directory tests/migrations/ exists and is empty except for this non-existent file marker. The read-only context directory src/migrations/ contains the migration infrastructure: migrate.ts defines the MigrationManager class with run(direction, version) method that applies or reverts migrations, and schema.ts provides getSchemaVersion() and getSchemaDiff() utilities for schema state inspection. The claim under test is: 'the database migration rollback restores the schema to the previous version without data loss.'

The architect_lead that dispatched you has communicated the following: "Red-phase test authoring for the migration rollback claim. Write boundary is tests/migrations/rollback.test.ts. The falsification criterion is that the test must fail if schema state differs from the previous version or if data is lost. Coverage target is the rollback execution path and post-rollback schema verification. Read-only context is src/migrations/. Stop condition: red tests authored and verified failing. Chaining budget is 0 — no sub-dispatch permitted."

Your task is to author a failing test for the database migration rollback claim. The test must be written to tests/migrations/rollback.test.ts. It is a complex multi-aspect claim with two distinct coverage targets: (1) schema restoration — after rollback, the schema version should match the previous version's schema, verified by comparing schema state; (2) data preservation — after rollback, all data that existed in the previous schema version should still be present, unless that data was inherently tied to a schema element that was removed as part of the migration (in which case data loss is acceptable only for data associated with removed schema elements). The test must falsify both aspects: it should fail if the schema version does not match the expected previous version, and it should fail if data is lost in a way that violates the data preservation guarantee.

The test will be complex because it needs to: establish a baseline with some data at the current schema version, run a rollback to the previous version, then verify both the schema state and the data state. Since the test is authored in the red phase, it should be written to expect the correct behavior (schema restored to previous version, no data loss beyond explicit migration removals), and will therefore fail against a stub implementation.

Because your chaining budget is 0, you cannot sub-dispatch any tasks. You must handle all aspects of this complex test authoring directly: reading the migration infrastructure to understand how rollbacks work and how schema versions are tracked, understanding the data preservation semantics, designing the test scenario with appropriate setup and assertions, writing the test file, and running it to verify it fails. Do not use the Task tool for any purpose.

Your output must conform to the standard test return schema: { "test_file": "tests/migrations/rollback.test.ts", "status": "red", "failing": true, "verification": "test run output confirming failure" }. Stop when you have authored the test, verified it fails, and returned the standard schema output.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling of a complex multi-aspect claim (schema restoration and data preservation) without sub-dispatch under zero budget
